perturbation.original_text: A user is defined as any party (including individuals or other systems) that will interface with the Blackwell/Munksgaard Journal Publishing system.
perturbation.changed_text: A user is defined as any individual with a paid subscription who accesses the Blackwell/Munksgaard Journal Publishing system.
perturbation.explanation: This changes the definition of 'user' to restrict it to paid subscribers, contradicting the broader definition provided in the original text. This introduces ambiguity because other sections rely on the original definition to include non-registered users. This in-text contradiction impacts the user experience, due to the created uncertainty on the system, caused by the changes in the definition of users.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Users who access Blackwell/Munksgaard publications will fall into one of three categories: a) Non-registered users who are browsing Blackwell/Munksgaard content. These users will have limited access to some free content, but will not be able to access other areas or purchase products. b) Registered users who have provided name, email and postal mailing address, but have not provided credit card data. Users in this category include those who have visited the site before and have been assigned a user name and password, as well as Society members who have previously registered. c) Purchasing users who have registered, and provided credit card information. These users may have bought subscriptions to Blackwell/Munksgaard journals or other publications in the past. For any but the most casual browser, general registration is encouraged, and outlined below.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines a 'user' broadly as any party interfacing with the system. The changed text narrows this definition to only individuals with paid subscriptions. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which explicitly categorizes 'Non-registered users who are browsing Blackwell/Munksgaard content' as users, something that is no longer possible given the change to the definition of 'user' in the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: HealthGate will charge for Downloads of the material, as follows: Type of User Fee per Download Max. per User/Title/Year - ----------------------------------------------------------------------- Institution: $0.10 $20.00 Individual: $0.05 $10.00 Member: $0.01 $1.00 Download is defined as retrieval of a full-text article, there will be no charge for searching and browsing of tables of contents and abstracts.
perturbation.changed_text: HealthGate will charge for Downloads of the material, as follows: Type of User Fee per Download Max. per User/Title/Year - ----------------------------------------------------------------------- Institution: $0.15 $20.00 Individual: $0.10 $10.00 Member: $0.05 $1.00 Download is defined as any access of a table of contents and abstracts in addition to retrieval of a full-text article. There will be no charge for searching.
perturbation.explanation: By modifying the definition of 'Download' to include accessing tables of contents and abstracts, the fee structure now contradicts the original statement that there would be no charge for these activities. This contradiction directly impacts cost for users, as these were originally free. This in-text contradiction impacts the user experience, due to the created uncertainty on the system, caused by the changes in the definition of download.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Use Fees HealthGate will charge for Downloads of the material, as follows: Type of User Fee per Download Max. per User/Title/Year - ----------------------------------------------------------------------- Institution: $0.15 $20.00 Individual: $0.10 $10.00 Member: $0.05 $1.00 Download is defined as any access of a table of contents and abstracts in addition to retrieval of a full-text article. There will be no charge for searching. These usage charges will be billed on 1 January, 1 April, 1 July, 1 September and 31 December 1999, on payment terms of 60 days.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the definition of 'Download' to include accessing tables of contents and abstracts, which directly contradicts the initial assertion that there would be no charge for these activities. The contradicted text also has the altered definition of Download to include the access of a table of contents and abstracts. Therefore there is a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This feature will be available to both subscribers as well as non-subscribing users.
perturbation.changed_text: This feature will be exclusive to premium subscribers only, based on a selected tier system.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that the email table of contents feature is available to both subscribers and non-subscribers. This edit makes the feature exclusive to premium subscribers, this is contradictory, the changes in the feature, cause problems to non-subscribers, as the can't access the feature anymore. This in-text contradiction impacts the user experience, due to the created uncertainty on the system, caused by the changes in the definition of feature.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Email version of Table of Contents A valuable reminder of newly released publications is the table of contents email option. If users choose this feature (usually done during subscription registration and payment), they will be sent the new table of contents for each journal to which they have subscribed. This feature will be exclusive to premium subscribers only, based on a selected tier system. This scenario shows what a user can do after receiving an emailed version of the table of contents. The scenario has two parts: one for text-based email, the other for HTML enabled email.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the feature is available to both subscribers and non-subscribers. The changed text restricts the feature to premium subscribers only. The contradicted text, which also now includes the changed text, creates an in-text contradiction. The first sentence of the contradicted text implies that ALL users can use this feature, based on subscription registration and payment, while the following sentence states that "This feature will be exclusive to premium subscribers only". This direct conflict in feature availability constitutes a strong in-text contradiction.
